Chris Paul\xe2\x80\x99s Fast Hands and Gruesome Fingers - The New York Times

NY Times · by Scott Cacciola · March 22, 2017

Paul, the Clippers\xe2\x80\x99 ball-hawking guard, has paid a price \xe2\x80\x94 multiple injuries to his fingers \xe2\x80\x94 for collecting more steals than any other active N.B.A. player.

Clippers guard Chris Paul broke his right hand last April and plays with 16 pins and a metal plate embedded in his palm. He shoots with his right hand.Credit...Alexis Cuarezma for The New York Times

By

  • March 22, 2017

LOS ANGELES \xe2\x80\x94 Chris Paul examined his crooked knuckles and the surgical scars that form tiny road maps on his hands. He has torn various ligaments in his thumbs and has broken bones in his fingers. He has worn casts and splints. His hands, though, are the indispensable tools of his trade, no matter how gruesome they look.

\xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve got the worst fingers,\xe2\x80\x9d he said.

Paul, the starting point guard for the Los Angeles Clippers, long ago established himself as one of the N.B.A.\xe2\x80\x99s premier defenders. He has collected more steals than any other active player in the league: 1,892 and counting. A nine-time All-Star, he clutters passing lanes. He strips dribbles like paint thinner. Doc Rivers, his coach, has concluded that Paul has the best hands he has ever seen.

\xe2\x80\x9cGifted,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said during an interview in January. \xe2\x80\x9cHis hands are so damn quick.\xe2\x80\x9d

Three days after Rivers offered this assessment, Paul paid the price for possessing such gifts. In a game against the Oklahoma City Thunder, he jammed his left thumb fighting through a screen, tearing his ulnar collateral ligament \xe2\x80\x94 not to be confused with his radial collateral ligament, the one he tore on his right thumb training for the Olympics in 2012.

Few people on the planet have firsthand knowledge of the difference between R.C.L. and U.C.L. tears. Paul is one of them. (They both hurt, he said.)

As for his most recent injury, Paul missed 14 games before he returned late last month. Even now, with the Clippers vying for playoff position, he is trying to regain his feel for the game.

\xe2\x80\x9cI see plays all the time where I know I can reach for the ball and I know I can steal it,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut it\xe2\x80\x99s like risk versus reward: Do I go for it?\xe2\x80\x9d

Such is life for Paul, who cannot help putting his hands in harm\xe2\x80\x99s way. He tore ligaments in his right middle finger in March 2010. He fractured his left index finger in October 2015. He broke his right hand last April, and he continues to play with 16 pins and a metal plate embedded in his palm. He shoots with that hand.

In the process, he has become something of an expert on hands for colleagues. After J. R. Smith of the Cleveland Cavaliers fractured his right thumb in December, Paul called him to offer tips on his rehabilitation. Whenever Paul himself jams a thumb or sprains a finger, the first person he usually hears from is Matt Barnes, a former teammate who now plays for the Golden State Warriors.

\xe2\x80\x9cBecause he gets his fingers hit all the time,\xe2\x80\x9d said Paul, who is averaging 17.6 points, 9.2 assists and 1.9 steals a game. \xe2\x80\x9cHe understands.\xe2\x80\x9d

The challenge for Paul is that he relies on his hands. He is not, by his own admission, one of the league\xe2\x80\x99s most awesome physical specimens. He is 6 feet tall and weighs 175 pounds. He is not particularly fast and does not jump especially high. But he has exceptional reflexes and a knack for anticipating plays.

\xe2\x80\x9cHe could have been a boxer,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said. \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t know if he could have taken a punch, but he could have landed some for sure.\xe2\x80\x9d

Growing up in North Carolina, Paul worked to develop hand speed and coordination. He was almost never without a ball, he said. He also watched the game constantly, studying how other players dribbled and passed, and all the angles they used to create space. He determined that there were a finite number of possibilities.

\xe2\x80\x9cNot being the most athletic player,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve always had to understand where you\xe2\x80\x99re going with the ball.\xe2\x80\x9d

He recorded his first N.B.A. steal before he scored his first N.B.A. points, intercepting an errant pass before racing away for a layup. His professional career was less than four minutes old. In 2008, he set an N.B.A. record by going 108 straight games with at least one steal.

\xe2\x80\x9cI think that one\xe2\x80\x99s going to be hard to break,\xe2\x80\x9d he said.

Jamal Crawford, his teammate, said that Paul had an encyclopedic knowledge of his opponents thanks to his experience and his tireless analysis of game film. \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99s ahead of the play,\xe2\x80\x9d Crawford said.

Metta World Peace, a veteran forward with the Los Angeles Lakers and a former defensive player of the year, cited Paul\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9chunger.\xe2\x80\x9d Few players, for example, are more skilled at recovering the ball if it happens to get stolen away.

\xe2\x80\x9cOh, I\xe2\x80\x99m getting it back,\xe2\x80\x9d Paul said. \xe2\x80\x9cI want it. I want it.\xe2\x80\x9d

But Paul said that no one had better hands than Brevin Knight, a point guard who retired in 2009 and now works as an analyst for the Memphis Grizzlies\xe2\x80\x99 television broadcasts. As a kid, Knight knew that the only way he was going to earn his way onto the prime playground courts near his home in East Orange, N.J., was by playing great defense.

\xe2\x80\x9cBeing smaller, you have to figure out a way for people to want to pick you so you\xe2\x80\x99re not just sitting there on the side watching,\xe2\x80\x9d he said in a telephone interview. \xe2\x80\x9cIt stuck with me from there.\xe2\x80\x9d

Knight described himself as a \xe2\x80\x9ctendency person.\xe2\x80\x9d He could study an opponent for two possessions, he said, and get a feel for what that player liked to do with the ball. Paul is the same way.

\xe2\x80\x9cHe has the ability to look as though the he is not engaged defensively, but he\xe2\x80\x99s really there,\xe2\x80\x9d Knight said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s his way of luring the offensive guy into thinking, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I\xe2\x80\x99ve got an easy move here.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

In retirement, Knight has found that his good hands \xe2\x80\x94 along with excellent peripheral vision \xe2\x80\x94 are still useful when he broadcasts games. Occasionally, he said, a loose ball will rocket toward the courtside table where he and Pete Pranica, the Grizzlies\xe2\x80\x99 play-by-play voice, are calling the action. Knight tries to save Pranica first, he said, and the courtside monitor second.

\xe2\x80\x9cBecause these teams have enough money,\xe2\x80\x9d Knight said, \xe2\x80\x9cthey can replace a monitor.\xe2\x80\x9d

Rivers knows about defense, too. During his 13-year playing career, he came away with 1,563 steals and the mangled instruments of his profession.

\xe2\x80\x9cI mean, honestly, look at my fingers,\xe2\x80\x9d said Rivers, who stretched his hands before him and stared at them as if they had been assembled by Picasso.

Rivers was constantly putting his hands in danger. He described himself as a \xe2\x80\x9creacher.\xe2\x80\x9d He reached for loose balls. He reached for deflections. And he reached into the midsections of opposing point guards, committing an average of one foul for every nine minutes of playing time. Paul, on the other hand, goes about 14 minutes between fouls.

\xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m amazed how clean he gets steals in traffic,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said.

Not that he is immune from workplace hazards. One of Paul\xe2\x80\x99s more devastating injuries occurred last April, against the Portland Trail Blazers in the first round of the playoffs. As Portland\xe2\x80\x99s Gerald Henderson drove for a layup, Paul used his left hand to strip the ball away from Henderson. The problem was that Paul entangled his right hand in the back of Henderson\xe2\x80\x99s jersey.

He led a fast break in the other direction, but the damage was done: He had broken the third metacarpal in his right palm. The Clippers lost the next two games and the series without him.

\xe2\x80\x9cRight when he did it, you could see me react on the sideline,\xe2\x80\x9d said Rivers, who did not react well. \xe2\x80\x9cI knew what it was. Because I had done the exact same thing when I was playing.\xe2\x80\x9d

This season has been another exercise in perseverance for Paul, who sprained his left thumb at a preseason practice, which was bad enough. Then, in January, he tried to squeeze past that screen against Oklahoma City \xe2\x80\x94 a collision made worse by the Thunder\xe2\x80\x99s Russell Westbrook, who tried to draw a foul by jumping into Paul.

\xe2\x80\x9cI tried to shake it out because I\xe2\x80\x99ve jammed my thumb a thousand times,\xe2\x80\x9d Paul said. \xe2\x80\x9cI thought it would calm down. But I could see how swollen it was. I said: \xe2\x80\x98I tore it. I know I did.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

After spending several weeks squeezing a tiny rubber ball to rebuild the strength in his thumb, Paul has since returned to the court. He would like to stay there.

\xe2\x80\x9cJust trying to keep my hands out of the way,\xe2\x80\x9d he said.

A version of this article appears in print on , Section B, Page 9 of the New York edition with the headline: The Gnarliest Defender

hey both hurt, he said.) Paul, a nine- time All- Star, is among the N.B.A.\xe2\x80\x99s premier defenders with 1,892 steals. Credit... Alexis Cuarezma for The New York Times As for his most recent injury, Paul missed 14 games before he returned late last month. Even now, with the Clippers vying for playoff position, he is trying to regain his feel for the game. \xe2\x80\x9cI see plays all the time where I know I can reach for the ball and I know I can steal it,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut it\xe2\x80\x99s like risk versus reward: Do I go for it?\xe2\x80\x9d Such is life for Paul, who cannot help putting his hands in harm\xe2\x80\x99s way. He tore ligaments in his right middle finger in March 2010. He fractured his left index finger in October 2015. He broke his right hand last April, and he continues to play with 16 pins and a metal plate embedded in his palm. He shoots with that hand. In the process, he has become something of an expert on hands for colleagues. After J. R. Smith of the Cleveland Cavaliers fractured his right thumb in December, Paul called him to offer tips on his rehabilitation. Whenever Paul himself jams a thumb or sprains a finger, the first person he usually hears from is Matt Barnes, a former teammate who now plays for the Golden State Warriors. Paul (3) reaching for a ball held by the Bucks\xe2\x80\x99 Giannis Antetokounmpo. \xe2\x80\x9cHis hands are so damn quick,\xe2\x80\x9d said Doc Rivers, Paul\xe2\x80\x99s coach. Credit... Patrick T. Fallon for The New York Times \xe2\x80\x9cBecause he gets his fingers hit all the time,\xe2\x80\x9d said Paul, who is averaging 17.6 points, 9.2 assists and 1.9 steals a game. \xe2\x80\x9cHe understands.\xe2\x80\x9d The challenge for Paul is that he relies on his hands. He is not, by his own admission, one of the league\xe2\x80\x99s most awesome physical specimens. He is 6 feet tall and weighs 175 pounds. He is not particularly fast and does not jump especially high. But he has exceptional reflexes and a knack for anticipating plays. \xe2\x80\x9cHe could have been a boxer,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said. \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t know if he could have taken a punch, but he could have landed some for sure.\xe2\x80\x9d Growing up in North Carolina, Paul worked to develop hand speed and coordination. He was almost never without a ball, he said. He also watched the game constantly, studying how other players dribbled and passed, and all the angles they used to create space. He determined that there were a finite number of possibilities. Paul wore a splint on his left hand on March 15 during a game against the Bucks. \xe2\x80\x9cI see plays all the time where I know I can reach for the ball and I know I can steal it,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut it\xe2\x80\x99s like risk versus reward: Do I go for it?\xe2\x80\x9d Credit... Patrick T. Fallon for The New York Times \xe2\x80\x9cNot being the most athletic player,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve always had to understand where you\xe2\x80\x99re going with the ball.\xe2\x80\x9d He recorded his first N.B.A. steal before he scored his first N.B.A. points, intercepting an errant pass before racing away for a layup. His professional career was less than four minutes old. In 2008, he set an N.B.A. record by going 108 straight games with at least one steal. \xe2\x80\x9cI think that one\xe2\x80\x99s going to be hard to break,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. Jamal Crawford, his teammate, said that Paul had an encyclopedic knowledge of his opponents thanks to his experience and his tireless analysis of game film. \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99s ahead of the play,\xe2\x80\x9d Crawford said. Metta World Peace, a veteran forward with the Los Angeles Lakers and a former defensive player of the year, cited Paul\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9chunger.\xe2\x80\x9d Few players, for example, are more skilled at recovering the ball if it happens to get stolen away. \xe2\x80\x9cOh, I\xe2\x80\x99m getting it back,\xe2\x80\x9d Paul said. \xe2\x80\x9cI want it. I want it.\xe2\x80\x9d But Paul said that no one had better hands than Brevin Knight, a point guard who retired in 2009 and now works as an analyst for the Memphis Grizzlies\xe2\x80\x99 television broadcasts. As a kid, Knight knew that the only way he was going to earn his way onto the prime playground courts near his home in East Orange, N.J., was by playing great defense. \xe2\x80\x9cBeing smaller, you have to figure out a way for people to want to pick you so you\xe2\x80\x99re not just sitting there on the side watching,\xe2\x80\x9d he said in a telephone interview. \xe2\x80\x9cIt stuck with me from there.\xe2\x80\x9d Knight described himself as a \xe2\x80\x9ctendency person.\xe2\x80\x9d He could study an opponent for two possessions, he said, and get a feel for what that player liked to do with the ball. Paul is the same way. \xe2\x80\x9cHe has the ability to look as though the he is not engaged defensively, but he\xe2\x80\x99s really there,\xe2\x80\x9d Knight said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s his way of luring the offensive guy into thinking, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I\xe2\x80\x99ve got an easy move here.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Paul, center, at 6 feet and 175 pounds, is small for an N.B.A. player, but has exceptional reflexes and a knack for anticipating plays. Credit... Patrick T. Fallon for The New York Times In retirement, Knight has found that his good hands along with excellent peripheral vision are still useful when he broadcasts games. Occasionally, he said, a loose ball will rocket toward the courtside table where he and Pete Pranica, the Grizzlies\xe2\x80\x99 play- by- play voice, are calling the action. Knight tries to save Pranica first, he said, and the courtside monitor second. \xe2\x80\x9cBecause these teams have enough money,\xe2\x80\x9d Knight said, \xe2\x80\x9cthey can replace a monitor.\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers knows about defense, too. During his 13- year playing career, he came away with 1,563 steals and the mangled instruments of his profession. \xe2\x80\x9cI mean, honestly, look at my fingers,\xe2\x80\x9d said Rivers, who stretched his hands before him and stared at them as if they had been assembled by Picasso. Rivers was constantly putting his hands in danger. He described himself as a \xe2\x80\x9creacher.\xe2\x80\x9d He reached for loose balls. He reached for deflections. And he reached into the midsections of opposing point guards, committing an average of one foul for every nine minutes of playing time. Paul, on the other hand, goes about 14 minutes between fouls. Paul tore the ulnar collateral ligament in his left thumb this season and fractured his left index finger in October 2015. Credit... Alexis Cuarezma for The New York Times \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m amazed how clean he gets steals in traffic,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said. Not that he is immune from workplace hazards. One of Paul\xe2\x80\x99s more devastating injuries occurred last April, against the Portland Trail Blazers in the first round of the playoffs. As Portland\xe2\x80\x99s Gerald Henderson drove for a layup, Paul used his left hand to strip the ball away from Henderson. The problem was that Paul entangled his right hand in the back of Henderson\xe2\x80\x99s jersey. He led a fast break in the other direction, but the damage was done: He had broken the third metacarpal in his right palm. The Clippers lost the next two games and the series without him. \xe2\x80\x9cRight when he did it, you could see me react on the sideline,\xe2\x80\x9d said Rivers, who did not react well. \xe2\x80\x9cI knew what it was. Because I had done the exact same thing when I was playing.\xe2\x80\x9d This season has been another exercise in perseverance for Paul, who sprained his left thumb at a preseason practice, which was bad enough. Then, in January, he tried to squeeze past that screen against Oklahoma City a collision made worse by the Thunder\xe2\x80\x99s Russell Westbrook, who tried to draw a foul by jumping into Paul. \xe2\x80\x9cI tried to shake it out because I\xe2\x80\x99ve jammed my thumb a thousand times,\xe2\x80\x9d Paul said. \xe2\x80\x9cI thought it would calm down. But I could see how swollen it was. I said: \xe2\x80\x98I tore it. I know I did.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d After spending several weeks squeezing a tiny rubber ball to rebuild the strength in his thumb, Paul has since returned to the court. He would like to stay there. \xe2\x80\x9cJust trying to keep my hands out of the way,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. A version of this article appears in print on , Section B, Page 9 of the New York edition with the headline: The Gnarliest Defender See more on: Los Angeles Clippers Clippers guard Chris Paul broke his right hand last April and plays with 16 pins and a metal plate embedded in his palm. He shoots with his right hand. Credit... Alexis Cuarezma for The New York Times Paul, the Clippers\xe2\x80\x99 ball- hawking guard, has paid a price multiple injuries to his fingers for collecting more steals than any other active N.B.A. player. Clippers guard Chris Paul broke his right hand last April and plays with 16 pins and a metal plate embedded in his palm. He shoots with his right hand. Credit... Alexis Cuarezma for The New York Times By March 22, 2017 LOS ANGELES Chris Paul examined his crooked knuckles and the surgical scars that form tiny road maps on his hands. He has torn various ligaments in his thumbs and has broken bones in his fingers. He has worn casts and splints. His hands, though, are the indispensable tools of his trade, no matter how gruesome they look. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve got the worst fingers,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. Paul, the starting point guard for the Los Angeles Clippers, long ago established himself as one of the N.B.A.\xe2\x80\x99s premier defenders. He has collected more steals than any other active player in the league: 1,892 and counting. A nine- time All- Star, he clutters passing lanes. He strips dribbles like paint thinner. Doc Rivers, his coach, has concluded that Paul has the best hands he has ever seen. \xe2\x80\x9cGifted,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said during an interview in January. \xe2\x80\x9cHis hands are so damn quick.\xe2\x80\x9d Three days after Rivers offered this assessment, Paul paid the price for possessing such gifts. In a game against the Oklahoma City Thunder, he jammed his left thumb fighting through a screen, tearing his ulnar collateral ligament not to be confused with his radial collateral ligament, the one he tore on his right thumb training for the Olympics in 2012. Few people on the planet have firsthand knowledge of the difference between R.C.L. and U.C.L. tears. Paul is one of them. (They both hurt, he said.) Paul, a nine- time All- Star, is among the N.B.A.\xe2\x80\x99s premier defenders with 1,892 steals. Credit... Alexis Cuarezma for The New York Times As for his most recent injury, Paul missed 14 games before he returned late last month. Even now, with the Clippers vying for playoff position, he is trying to regain his feel for the game. \xe2\x80\x9cI see plays all the time where I know I can reach for the ball and I know I can steal it,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut it\xe2\x80\x99s like risk versus reward: Do I go for it?\xe2\x80\x9d Such is life for Paul, who cannot help putting his hands in harm\xe2\x80\x99s way. He tore ligaments in his right middle finger in March 2010. He fractured his left index finger in October 2015. He broke his right hand last April, and he continues to play with 16 pins and a metal plate embedded in his palm. He shoots with that hand. In the process, he has become something of an expert on hands for colleagues. After J. R. Smith of the Cleveland Cavaliers fractured his right thumb in December, Paul called him to offer tips on his rehabilitation. Whenever Paul himself jams a thumb or sprains a finger, the first person he usually hears from is Matt Barnes, a former teammate who now plays for the Golden State Warriors. Paul (3) reaching for a ball held by the Bucks\xe2\x80\x99 Giannis Antetokounmpo. \xe2\x80\x9cHis hands are so damn quick,\xe2\x80\x9d said Doc Rivers, Paul\xe2\x80\x99s coach. Credit... Patrick T. Fallon for The New York Times \xe2\x80\x9cBecause he gets his fingers hit all the time,\xe2\x80\x9d said Paul, who is averaging 17.6 points, 9.2 assists and 1.9 steals a game. \xe2\x80\x9cHe understands.\xe2\x80\x9d The challenge for Paul is that he relies on his hands. He is not, by his own admission, one of the league\xe2\x80\x99s most awesome physical specimens. He is 6 feet tall and weighs 175 pounds. He is not particularly fast and does not jump especially high. But he has exceptional reflexes and a knack for anticipating plays. \xe2\x80\x9cHe could have been a boxer,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said. \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t know if he could have taken a punch, but he could have landed some for sure.\xe2\x80\x9d Growing up in North Carolina, Paul worked to develop hand speed and coordination. He was almost never without a ball, he said. He also watched the game constantly, studying how other players dribbled and passed, and all the angles they used to create space. He determined that there were a finite number of possibilities. Paul wore a splint on his left hand on March 15 during a game against the Bucks. \xe2\x80\x9cI see plays all the time where I know I can reach for the ball and I know I can steal it,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut it\xe2\x80\x99s like risk versus reward: Do I go for it?\xe2\x80\x9d Credit... Patrick T. Fallon for The New York Times \xe2\x80\x9cNot being the most athletic player,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve always had to understand where you\xe2\x80\x99re going with the ball.\xe2\x80\x9d He recorded his first N.B.A. steal before he scored his first N.B.A. points, intercepting an errant pass before racing away for a layup. His professional career was less than four minutes old. In 2008, he set an N.B.A. record by going 108 straight games with at least one steal. \xe2\x80\x9cI think that one\xe2\x80\x99s going to be hard to break,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. Jamal Crawford, his teammate, said that Paul had an encyclopedic knowledge of his opponents thanks to his experience and his tireless analysis of game film. \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99s ahead of the play,\xe2\x80\x9d Crawford said. Metta World Peace, a veteran forward with the Los Angeles Lakers and a former defensive player of the year, cited Paul\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9chunger.\xe2\x80\x9d Few players, for example, are more skilled at recovering the ball if it happens to get stolen away. \xe2\x80\x9cOh, I\xe2\x80\x99m getting it back,\xe2\x80\x9d Paul said. \xe2\x80\x9cI want it. I want it.\xe2\x80\x9d But Paul said that no one had better hands than Brevin Knight, a point guard who retired in 2009 and now works as an analyst for the Memphis Grizzlies\xe2\x80\x99 television broadcasts. As a kid, Knight knew that the only way he was going to earn his way onto the prime playground courts near his home in East Orange, N.J., was by playing great defense. \xe2\x80\x9cBeing smaller, you have to figure out a way for people to want to pick you so you\xe2\x80\x99re not just sitting there on the side watching,\xe2\x80\x9d he said in a telephone interview. \xe2\x80\x9cIt stuck with me from there.\xe2\x80\x9d Knight described himself as a \xe2\x80\x9ctendency person.\xe2\x80\x9d He could study an opponent for two possessions, he said, and get a feel for what that player liked to do with the ball. Paul is the same way. \xe2\x80\x9cHe has the ability to look as though the he is not engaged defensively, but he\xe2\x80\x99s really there,\xe2\x80\x9d Knight said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s his way of luring the offensive guy into thinking, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I\xe2\x80\x99ve got an easy move here.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Paul, center, at 6 feet and 175 pounds, is small for an N.B.A. player, but has exceptional reflexes and a knack for anticipating plays. Credit... Patrick T. Fallon for The New York Times In retirement, Knight has found that his good hands along with excellent peripheral vision are still useful when he broadcasts games. Occasionally, he said, a loose ball will rocket toward the courtside table where he and Pete Pranica, the Grizzlies\xe2\x80\x99 play- by- play voice, are calling the action. Knight tries to save Pranica first, he said, and the courtside monitor second. \xe2\x80\x9cBecause these teams have enough money,\xe2\x80\x9d Knight said, \xe2\x80\x9cthey can replace a monitor.\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers knows about defense, too. During his 13- year playing career, he came away with 1,563 steals and the mangled instruments of his profession. \xe2\x80\x9cI mean, honestly, look at my fingers,\xe2\x80\x9d said Rivers, who stretched his hands before him and stared at them as if they had been assembled by Picasso. Rivers was constantly putting his hands in danger. He described himself as a \xe2\x80\x9creacher.\xe2\x80\x9d He reached for loose balls. He reached for deflections. And he reached into the midsections of opposing point guards, committing an average of one foul for every nine minutes of playing time. Paul, on the other hand, goes about 14 minutes between fouls. Paul tore the ulnar collateral ligament in his left thumb this season and fractured his left index finger in October 2015. Credit... Alexis Cuarezma for The New York Times \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m amazed how clean he gets steals in traffic,\xe2\x80\x9d Rivers said. Not that he is immune from workplace hazards. One of Paul\xe2\x80\x99s more devastating injuries occurred last April, against the Portland Trail Blazers in the first round of the playoffs. As Portland\xe2\x80\x99s Gerald Henderson drove for a layup, Paul used his left hand to strip the ball away from Henderson. The problem was that Paul entangled his right hand in the back of Henderson\xe2\x80\x99s jersey. He led a fast break in the other direction, but the damage was done: He had broken the third metacarpal in his right palm. The Clippers lost the next two games and the series without him. \xe2\x80\x9cRight when he did it, you could see me react on the sideline,\xe2\x80\x9d said Rivers, who did not react well. \xe2\x80\x9cI knew what it was. Because I had done the exact same thing when I was playing.\xe2\x80\x9d This season has been another exercise in perseverance for Paul, who sprained his left thumb at a preseason practice, which was bad enough. Then, in January, he tried to squeeze past that screen against Oklahoma City a collision made worse by the Thunder\xe2\x80\x99s Russell Westbrook, who tried to draw a foul by jumping into Paul. \xe2\x80\x9cI tried to shake it out because I\xe2\x80\x99ve jammed my thumb a thousand times,\xe2\x80\x9d Paul said. \xe2\x80\x9cI thought it would calm down. But I could see how swollen it was. I said: \xe2\x80\x98I tore it. I know I did.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d After spending several weeks squeezing a tiny rubber ball to rebuild the strength in his thumb, Paul has since returned to the court. He would like to stay there. \xe2\x80\x9cJust trying to keep my hands out of the way,\xe2\x80\x9d he said.
